data.title: Alpha Picks Weekly Market Recap
data.description:
‘Boring is Back’: Defensive Sectors Shine

‘Boring is back,’ according to Jay Woods, chief global strategist at Freedom Capital Markets. As the market gravitated toward defensive stocks amidst the volatility, a shift to a defensive posture is underscored by the recent outperformance of the Consumer Staples (XLP) and Utilities sectors (XLU) over the last month. Woods emphasized that investors are being cautious and seeking stability in the face of economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions. Consumer confidence took a notable hit this week, as the latest data revealed a significant decline following escalating trade tensions and President Trump's announcement of new tariffs.

The Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence Index slumped to 98.3 in February 2025, missing the 102.7 Bloomberg consensus and weakening from 105.3 in January. The dip reflected heightened economic uncertainty among consumers. Analysts attribute this drop to concerns surrounding price stability and the potential impact of tariffs on household expenses. 12-month inflation expectations jumped to 6% from 5.2%.

As consumer sentiment weakens, there’s increased apprehension about reduced spending power, which could ultimately weigh on economic growth in the coming months. The lingering uncertainty and fear is prompting investors to seek safer asset classes, contributing to further volatility across financial markets.

Source Link

The current market environment shows signs indicative of a potential correction. Factors contributing to this include the significant increase in market volatility driven by geopolitical tensions, such as President Trump's newly imposed tariffs. These tariffs are contributing to broader market angst, as evidenced by the S&P 500’s muted movement and the Nasdaq’s decline. The announcement of tariffs on Canadian, Mexican, and Chinese goods has heightened the uncertainty, leading investors to reevaluate risk assets and reassess their market positions.

As investors brace for uncertainty in navigating rough market conditions, Quant offers tools that simplify stock picking by analyzing financial data to highlight stocks with the strongest investment fundamentals. Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stock picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

Geopolitical Events

Geopolitical tensions have come to the forefront with the implementation of new tariff measures. President Trump imposed a 25% tariff on $1.2T of Canadian and Mexican imports and a 10% tariff on $550B of Chinese goods. These moves, aimed at addressing trade imbalances and curbing immigration, have heightened uncertainty in integrated North American supply chains and impacted sectors such as automotive and agriculture. The announcement of an additional 10% tariff on $550 billion of Chinese imports, effective March 4, has been a catalyst for market volatility. This policy shift by President Trump is intended to address the ongoing concerns over Chinese contributions to the U.S. opioid crisis, particularly regarding fentanyl production and trafficking. The implementation of these tariffs threatens to exacerbate trade tensions with China and has already led to a decline in market confidence.

The tariffs come amidst broader economic strategies aimed at reducing dependence on Chinese goods while reshaping the global trade landscape. Critics warn that these tariffs could escalate into a full-blown trade war, impacting not only bilateral trade but also global supply chains. Analysts are closely monitoring the situation, noting potential repercussions for inflation, consumer prices, and overall market sentiment. As businesses brace for increased costs, they may pass these expenses onto consumers, further fueling inflationary pressures.

Recent global equity markets have reacted mixed to the developments. While the Euro Stoxx 50 advanced 0.8%, buoyed by ECB rate-cut expectations, China’s Shanghai Composite dropped 2.9% amid mounting property sector defaults. The strength of the U.S. dollar has placed additional pressure on emerging market equities, with indices like the MSCI EM Currency Index reaching multi-year lows.

GICS Sector Performance

This week, defensive sectors continued to outperform amid tariff concerns. However, the Real Estate sector (XLRE) emerged as the top-performing following mortgage rates sliding to their lowest level in two months. The 30-year fixed rate averaged 6.76% as of February 27, down from 6.94% one year ago.

"The drop in mortgage rates, combined with modestly improving inventory, is an encouraging sign for consumers in the market to buy a home," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

Real Estate was the top-performing sector this week

Utilities and Consumer Staples have risen over the last month, driven by pricing power, the Technology sector (XLK) and Consumer Discretionary (XLY) continued their downtrend amid tariff tensions and potential supply chain issues.

Value Stocks vs. Growth Stocks

Value stocks outperformed growth equities, benefitting from a flight to safety amid geopolitical tensions. Despite the energy sector being “top detractors” for companies reliant on U.S. revenue exposure in Q4, companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), offering dividend safety, experienced gains throughout the week, which bolstered the performance of value stocks.

Value Stocks were the top-performing this week

In contrast, high-P/E growth stocks, like Tesla (TSLA), saw significant declines following a steep drop in European sales. The technology space also faced headwinds from heightened tariff concerns and supply chain disruptions. The preference for value stocks underscores investors’ emphasis on tangible earnings and defensive positioning in uncertain times.

Cryptocurrency

The digital asset market experienced a pronounced downturn, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) sliding over 25%, below $80,000 this week amid substantial ETF outflows. Secondary cryptocurrencies such as ETH-USD and SOL-USD were hit even harder, declining 31% and 42%, respectively, as investor enthusiasm for meme coins evaporated. The decline extended to crypto miners, with shares of MARA Holdings, Inc. (MARA) and Riot Platforms, Inc. (RIOT) experiencing significant declines, reflecting broad-based sell-offs across the sector.

A broader look reveals that the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies dipped to $2.68 trillion in February from $3.45 in January. That was a 22.5% contraction, the strongest monthly decline since June 2022. Bitcoin’s and Ethereum’s market cap stood at $1.63 and $0.26 trillion, respectively.

Currency

The Japanese yen weakened this week as the U.S. Dollar gained strength. The Japanese yen depreciated by 1.1%, despite ongoing speculation about a potential interest rate hike by the Bank of Japan. Emerging market currencies experienced significant pressure, with the Mexican peso dropping 2.3% and the South African rand falling 1.9%, highlighting their vulnerability to both global trade tensions and the strengthening dollar.

The USD continues to strengthen

The U.S. dollar index increased by 0.4% to reach 106.6 this week, as investors turned to safe-haven assets amidst escalating tensions over newly announced tariffs on imports from Canada, Mexico, and China. This strengthened demand for the dollar, indicating a flight to quality in uncertain economic conditions.

Chart of the Week – Will the Federal Reserve Step in?

Rising economic woes amid increasingly fragile consumer confidence have caused financial markets to adjust monetary policy expectations for this year. Federal Funds Futures now show 2.3 rate cuts priced in for 2025, up from just one cut over two weeks ago. This is the most dovish since the middle of December. Looking at the 2-year Treasury yield, another close proxy of monetary policy expectations, rates are down to 4.1% this week from 4.2% prior. That is the steepest drop since November.

Alpha Picks

What is Alpha Picks? Alpha Picks provides two data-driven stock picks a month, delivered straight to your inbox. Alpha Picks has collectively gained 144%, compared to the S&P 500’s 55%.

Uncertainty driven by rapid policy shifts, inflation, and interest rate concerns combined with weaker forward earnings for the broader market has contributed to a recent sell-off in some Alpha Picks stocks. In periods of volatility, it is normal to see investors take profits on the impressive gains many of these companies have achieved.

Economic Rundown

► FOMC – The current rate range has held steady at 4.25%-4.50%, with markets now pricing in approximately 58bps for Fed rate cuts in 2025 starting in July.

► Inflation — Tariff-driven inflation remains a concern following January’s CPI increase by 3% YoY, despite tenant rent data indicating shelter inflation could ease to approximately 2.5% by Q3.

► Labor Market – The upcoming March 7 jobs report is expected to reveal an increase of approximately 165K payrolls, following last week’s rise in initial claims to 219K.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

data.title: Veren: End Of An Era
data.description:
The last few articles on Veren (NYSE:VRN) concerned the end of the company, remaking itself from a zombie corporation headed to hereafter a cash flow juggernaut that is the future of the company now. In the process, the company made an investment grade rating for its debt. This is a huge improvement over what this management began with close to a decade ago. Now it is finally time to begin to deliver for shareholders. The last article noted what was happening on the first few steps the newly composed company was taking. The company sold some noncore acreage that was high cost and drilled a few experimental wells. Lost in the news was that the company has far better prospects going forward than it ever had before.

Fiscal Year 2025 Guidance Summary

Management has positioned the company to both grow and return cash to shareholders.

This is going to be the start of fiscal years where operations take precedence over reshuffling the acreage portfolio to improve the company situation. There will still be some deals made when the right deal comes by. But buying and selling will no longer be the main strategy.

Management now has what it wants, so shareholder returns have risen in importance for this investment grade level company.

This also means the start of fiscal years where the comparisons are unlikely to feature a material sale or purchase of acreage. That may make the comparison with the last fiscal year irrelevant, or at least very hard. But going forward, a track record will form and that will likely result in a better market valuation of the business.

Investors should expect that the shareholder returns will be a combination of share repurchases, a base dividend and possibly some special dividends from time to time. The total return from this program and slow business growth should be in the teens. An occasional opportunistic acquisition should raise that return on average a couple of percentage points.

Technology advances may factor into long-term returns as well, but that is harder to determine because technology advances often spread rapidly throughout the industry. That then often leads to selling price and margin adjustments.

Earnings

As can easily been seen from the shares outstanding comparison, one has to be extremely careful about comparing the annual data or even the quarterly data, as clearly some material changes were made.

(Note: Veren is a Canadian Company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Even though commodity prices were weaker in the latest fiscal year, management was able to hold quarterly adjusted funds flow relatively steady in the fourth quarter.

Net income took quite a dive in the latest fiscal year. But that could be due to the fact that many managements "reset the clock" conservatively for acquired existing production as it is often older technology and not expected to meet original assumptions as there are now more profitable ways to do things.

Furthermore, most managements will allocate acquisitions' costs to depreciating assets to the extent possible to protect cash flow, so there is cash flow to optimize the acquisition rather than paying some income taxes.

Now as new (often) lower cost production comes online, profits will climb, and depreciation will drop over time as that new lower cost production becomes more significant to the average cost.

Stock Price

The current stock price reflects all the uncertainty surrounding the acquisitions and dispositions that transformed the company. "A confused mind says no" and the stock price reflects that old saying.

Now that management has announced they have what they need, and the general strategy has changed from rearranging the assets to moving forward with what they have, the market will begin to make comparisons to determine a valuation. That valuation should gradually improve over time as management creates a track record in the future.

Not only does the history cover the stock price valuation from the end of the boom times around 2015, but also the turmoil the industry went through from 2016-2020. Through all of this, management transformed the company once it was clear that oil prices were not going to routinely hover around WTI $90 and higher as it did in the period before the chart coverage above began.

That is what caused the company to become a zombie corporation overnight.

The low-price earnings ratio and the dividend yield would indicate that if management performs as expected, there is some recovery potential to this stock price.

Much of that potential value depends upon conditions "when we get there" and to what extent the assets perform as management currently expects. Therefore, the upside potential likely has a wide range of possibilities.

But any management that has accomplished what has been done here is likely worth hanging onto until the story changes or the company is sold.

When this management took over back around 2015 (give or take) survival was a big issue. Now the debt is investment grade, which is a huge change from 10 years ago. Not many managements can do that.

Summary

As shown in the stock price chart, this turnaround situation was costly and maybe not worth the hold in the long term based upon what happened to the stock price during the turnaround. But the industry also went from a time of much higher oil prices to the prices now happening.

What happened with this company is a reason for diversification. Not all diversifications come off as planned. Certainly, the trials this industry went through (that bankrupted a lot of companies) from 2015-2020 are likely to not recur.

But now going forward, the low price-earnings ratio and the relatively high dividend yield reinforce the strong buy recommendation. This stock very likely has asymmetric return potential to the upside going forward. This is an excellent investment idea that would best be part of a basket given the experience in the past shown above.

This management has done a lot of right things for the company to survive that Mr. Market still punished the stock price compared to the boom times. But the corporate breakeven and asset performance have undergone a stunning transformation (as that investment grade rating shows).

Risks

Any upstream operation is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained decline (as what happened in 2015) can materially change the company outlook.

This management took a dire situation and turned the company into investment grade. Replacing key personnel with that kind of accomplishment resulting from loss of services could prove to be especially challenging. This type of management is hard enough to find the first time, let alone replace key personnel should that need to happen.

The acquired assets could fail to perform as planned, which would change the company outlook.

data.title: Beyond Meat: Expecting Roughly Flat Revenue Growth For 2025
data.description:
Beyond Meat (NASDAQ:BYND) appears to have largely stabilized its net revenues and has also made some progress with gross margin improvements. However, it fell short of its gross margin expectations for 2024 and expects roughly flat net revenue growth in 2025.

I estimate that Beyond Meat will have around $80 million in cash burn during 2025. It received $46.7 million in net proceeds during Q4 2024 through an at-the-market equity offering and now has $132 million in unrestricted cash.

Thus, Beyond Meat should theoretically have enough cash to get through 2025, although it may still issue more shares to keep its cash position healthy.

Beyond Meat has $1.15 billion in convertible notes maturing in March 2027 though, and those noteholders remain likely to end up with most of Beyond Meat's equity. The zero interest rate on the notes means that there is no real urgency to deal with those notes, though.

Beyond Meat's 2025 guidance was a bit weaker than what I had modeled before, so I am reducing its estimated value to $0.55 per share. This assumes a future balance sheet restructuring.

Results For Q4 2024

Beyond Meat reported $76.7 million in Q4 2024 net revenues, which was +4% year-over-year growth. This was a deceleration compared to the +8% year-over-year growth which it generated in Q3 2024, though.

Beyond Meat's gross margins also took a step back compared to recent quarters. It reported a 13.1% gross margin in Q4 2024, down from 17.7% in Q3 2024 and 14.7% in Q2 2024. This resulted in Beyond Meat's full-year gross margin ending up at 12.8%, lower than its updated guidance expectations for mid-teens gross margins in 2024.

Beyond Meat's initial guidance for 2024 called for mid-to-high teens gross margins, so it ended up around 4% below expectations for the year. The step back in Q4 2024 gross margins also reduces my confidence that it can get to 20+% gross margins in 2025.

The weaker-than-expected gross margins in Q4 2024 contributed to Beyond Meat generating negative $26 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q4 2024 and negative $102 million in adjusted EBITDA for the full year.

Beyond Meat's total cash (including restricted cash) went up to $146 million at the end of 2024, a roughly $11 million increase compared to the end of Q3, 2024. This was achieved by issuing new shares under its at-the-market program. Beyond Meat's outstanding share count went up by approximately 10.9 million shares during the quarter, and it generated net proceeds of approximately $46.7 million from its at-the-market program.

Cost Cutting

Beyond Meat has been cutting costs to try to get closer towards positive EBITDA. It recently approved a plan to reduce its workforce by 44 employees, which is around 6% of its global workforce or 17% of its global non-production workforce.

Beyond Meat mentioned that these cuts will save around $6 million in cash compensation when combined with changes to its executive leadership team and the elimination of some open positions.

Beyond Meat is also suspending operational activities in China and is reducing its workforce there by 20 employees. This is 95% of its China workforce, or 3% of its total global workforce. This move is expected to save up to $1 million in cash compensation in 2025.

2025 Outlook

Beyond Meat expects $320 million to $335 million in net revenues in 2025. This is essentially flat growth compared to 2024, with a -2% to +3% range. The suspension of its Chinese operations affects Beyond Meat's net revenue growth a bit, although it hasn't broken out how exactly much business it does in China.

It mentioned targeting 20% gross margins in 2025, although I believe there is some risk that it falls short of that target given that it fell around 4% short of expectations in 2024 and topped out at 17.7% gross margins in Q3 2024.

Operating expenses are expected to be reduced to $160 million to $180 million in 2025.

If Beyond Meat meets its guidance but falls a bit short on gross margin at 18%, then it may end up with around $80 million in cash burn for the year. If it meets its gross margin guidance, that would reduce its cash burn by around $7 million.

Beyond Meat's guidance is worse than what I had modeled before, since I had thought it could do mid-single digits revenue growth in 2025 as well as gross margins firmly in the 20s.

Notes On Valuation

I previously estimated Beyond Meat's value at $300 million based on 0.6x 2025 net revenues plus its projected cash on hand at the end of 2025.

I've revised its estimated value downward to approximately $265 million to reflect lower net revenues and increased cash burn for 2025 compared to my earlier projections, though.

This reduces Beyond Meat's estimated value to approximately $0.55 per share in my post balance sheet restructuring scenario. This assumes the convertible noteholders end up with close to 85% of Beyond Meat's equity. The convertible notes are trading at 17 cents on the dollar currently.

Beyond Meat is aiming for positive run-rate adjusted EBITDA by the end of 2026. That is only a few months before the notes become due, though, so even if Beyond Meat hits that target, the note principal will still likely be over 100x run-rate adjusted EBITDA.

While a balance sheet restructuring appears inevitable for Beyond Meat, there is also no real urgency to get a deal done since the notes pay zero interest. Thus, a balance sheet restructuring could have very soon, or it could even theoretically take until 2027 to get a deal done. The longer it takes to get a balance sheet restructuring done, the worse the results will likely be for existing shareholders, though.

Conclusion

Beyond Meat expects roughly flat net revenue growth in 2025 and some gross margin improvement, although it should be noted that it fell short of its 2024 gross margin expectations. It appears to expect close to $80 million in cash burn in 2025 and is aiming to reach positive run-rate EBITDA by the end of 2026.

Even if it reaches those targets, Beyond Meat is likely to be worth far less than the $1.15 billion in convertible notes that is due in March 2027. The balance sheet restructuring negotiations involving those notes will have major implications for the value of its existing stock.

I now estimate Beyond Meat's value at approximately $0.55 per share in a post-balance sheet restructuring scenario.

data.title: Couchbase: Stabilizing Growth Indicators Have Me Enthusiastic Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
With the markets displaying nervousness around turbulent macro conditions, persistent inflation, and geopolitical shifts, it has been rare for small/mid-cap rebound plays to enjoy rallies so far in the Q4 earnings season. And yet Couchbase (NASDAQ:BASE), the non-relational database company that is a competitor to the much better-known MongoDB (MDB), has pulled off this feat.

Over the past year, companies that have priced their software on a consumption basis have suffered as IT leaders have optimized their consumption to reduce budget pressures. Couchbase's Q4 results, however, showed a stabilization in consumption and revenue trends, boosting the stock by ~10% post-earnings. Still, Couchbase remains down more than ~30% over the past year, and amid modest valuation multiples, I think this is the right re-entry point for Couchbase as it has shown more reliable top-line trends.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Couchbase in December, when the stock was trading closer to $16 per share. Since then, Couchbase has seen revenue re-accelerate from a very poor showing in Q3, while the company's fully-managed offering, Capella, has continued to gain traction and contribute to the company's top-line expansion. As such, I'm upgrading Couchbase back up to a buy rating.

To me, these are the core drivers of the long-term bull case for Couchbase:

  • Huge $150 billion market for non-relational database products supported by secular tailwinds. The amount of "non relational" data, or unstructured data that can't fit neatly into rows and columns (such as video clips), is growing exponentially. At the same time, more and more companies are aiming to leverage and understand data in order to make more efficient decisions. This has led to Couchbase's estimate of a massive ~$150 billion TAM for its products.
  • Interoperable with public cloud services. The company's core growth lever is its fully managed Capella offering, which offers customers the choice of deploying Couchbase's databases in public cloud services like AWS instead of on their own data centers, fitting nicely into a hybrid cloud strategy.
  • Large ARR base and expanding customer trends. The company's ARR itself is greater than its annual revenue guidance for the current year, indicating that as long as Couchbase can sustain current consumption trends, it can hit its targets (and any new deals signed are extra icing on top). The company has shown a trend of rising ARR per customer.

Stay long here - it's a good time to buy Couchbase while it's still figuring out its direction, with the stock down meaningfully over the past year.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Couchbase's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Couchbase's revenue grew 10% y/y to $54.9 million, well ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $53.2 million (+6% y/y). Recall that in its Q3 release, Couchbase itself had guided to a major slowdown in revenue to just 5-7% y/y for Q4, and ended up coming in well above that guidance.

Of course, Couchbase's revenue is still decelerating - but it appears that the company's pace of deceleration has stabilized. Growth slowed three points relative to 13% y/y growth in Q3, but the company's outlook for FY26 (the year ending in January 2026) calls for growth to remain at the ~10% level (and its "mid-term" growth target still stands at 20%+, though we're unsure if the company is capable to climbing back to that pace of growth).

When questioned on the drivers for the meaningful outperformance versus its guidance on the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call, CEO Matt Cain responded as follows:

We're certainly proud of the execution across the company. Look, the result itself is great. What gives me a lot of confidence is the balance with which we delivered it. Certainly, we've been talking about the very high healthy pipeline of these large strategic accounts where we have the opportunity to emerge as a true platform winner. A big part of the quarter was executing against several of those. We've talked about the number of customers now over 1 million ARR. We crossed the $10 million threshold with a second customer. Simply put, I don't think you get to those levels if you're not truly viewed as a strategic platform provider.

And we're really excited to be able to talk about some of those results. But it wasn't just that. We saw strength with other expansions, Capella migrations, some excellent new logo lands in both Enterprise and Capella. We had one customer in particular that started out as a community edition that is already over the 1 million ARR mark in Capella. And so I think it was really balanced execution. And I think what we've been talking about is we've been building towards this and knew we had the pipeline to do it and it's great to have put this one up quite frankly."

On the ARR point, we note that the company added $17.6 million in net-new ARR within the quarter, which it cited as an all-time record:

What also is notable is the fact that Couchbase's ~$238 million ARR now exceeds its revenue guidance of $230 million for FY26. To me, this indicates that as long as consumption trends remain stable in FY26, the company should be able to comfortably beat-and-raise throughout the current year.

On the profitability front, Couchbase just managed to come in slightly under breakeven, with pro forma operating margins rising 8 points y/y to flat:

The company was able to achieve operating leverage primarily by driving down sales and marketing expenses as a percentage of revenue to 51%, from 59% in the year-ago Q4.

Valuation, risks and key takeaways

At Couchbase's current share prices near $18, the company trades at a $912.9 million market cap. After we net off the $147.1 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet, Couchbase's resulting enterprise value is $765.8 million.

Meanwhile, as previously mentioned, the company is guiding to $230 million in revenue this year (+10% y/y, and ARR indicates that there is likely upside to this conservative forecast), alongside a -5% pro forma operating margin (2 points of y/y improvement). The company is reiterating its expectation to re-accelerate to 20% y/y growth and positive FCF/pro forma operating profits in FY27.

This puts Couchbase's valuation at just 3.3x EV/FY26 revenue. We note that Couchbase trades below several other enterprise software peers that are showcasing high single digit/low-teens growth, despite the fact that there's likely upside to Couchbase's forecast.

Of course, investors should keep a pulse on the risks that Couchbase faces. Competition is a key one: MongoDB is growing faster than Couchbase, despite the size that its revenue scale is also ~10x larger. We also note the recent inconsistency in Couchbase's performance and its relative reliance on large-scale deals to meet its revenue targets, which may not materialize or be delayed if IT budgets continue to face high scrutiny.

All in all, however, I see more upside than downside in Couchbase when the stock sits at a modest ~3x forward revenue multiple, especially amid the company's reassuring guidance calling for stable growth in FY26 and acceleration in FY27. Stay long here.

data.title: Credo Technology: I'd Be Greedy Before The Earnings Release
data.description:
My Thesis Update

I initiated my coverage on Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (NASDAQ:CRDO) with a bullish rating in late January 2025, providing a reasoning that the stock's valuation - despite seemingly high multiples - isn't as expensive as many might think, and that CRDO may become a very big firm in the medium-term thanks to its massive TAM.

Unfortunately for my rating history on CRDO, the stock went through a meaningful correction since my call, while the broader market basically hasn't moved much:

Credo Technology is set to report its fiscal Q3 FY2025 results on March 4th, 2025, based on its IR data, and I think the stock is likely to react positively on the earnings print because the odds for another earnings beat are quite high, in my view. I'd buy more CRDO on Monday (March 3rd, 2025) if I had additional capital for that, so that's why I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating today.

My Reasoning

Credo Technology is a $9.2-billion market cap firm that provides "high-speed connectivity solutions for data infrastructure markets while addressing bandwidth problems in the fast-growing AI infrastructure sector."

We provide innovative, secure, high-speed connectivity solutions that deliver improved power efficiency as data rates and corresponding bandwidth requirements increase exponentially throughout the data infrastructure market.

Source: From CRDO's IR website

Nothing has changed in terms of the firm's financials since my last article, so I'll mainly focus on the description of my bullish take on CRDO's upcoming Q3.

Following a blowout earnings beat for Q2, when CRDO outperformed the EPS consensus by over 34%, Wall Street analysts massively raised their forecasts for Credo's Q3/Q4 FY2025 and FY2026:

I can't recall a company whose earnings expectations would be heightened that much in a matter of 3–6 months, taking into account that CRDO's business isn't really that cyclical in my understanding. I mean, it is cyclical - CRDO's main industry is Semiconductors - but the past few quarters have been truly impressive in the way Credo's operations scaled, so I don't understand why the Street initially had such a pessimistic view on the firm. Only after CRDO reported, some analysts rushed to change their minds:

Near term, we see the company driving a strong 2HFY25E (Jan’25Q plus Apr’25Q) driven by strong AI growth at Amazon Web Services, a key customer (and investor) at CRDO," Arya wrote in a note to clients. "Our prior cautious view was based on limited [total addressable market] assumptions for [active electrical cables], but CRDO’s earnings call addressed those concerns nicely, with 3x 10% customers (Microsoft, Amazon, Tesla), and growing momentum in other adjacent areas (optical DSP, line card PHY, retimer).

Source: Seeking Alpha, BofA's analyst Vivek Arya

And following those upgrades, the stock went up over 30% since its earnings print, but then sharply fell down, now trading at a 12% discount to its price at Q2 earnings release.

One of the drivers of Wall Street's massive upgrades was the management's updated outlook and guidance. For Q3, they anticipate revenue to be $115-125 million, so the midpoint should be ~66.7% above Q2 figure. The market consensus assumes $120.36 million for the quarter - just $360,000 above the mid-range. Given the beating of the past few quarters, I'm surprised to see such a little premium over CRDO's prospective revenue for Q3 - I'd rather focus on the higher end of the guided range, i.e. $125 million. So assuming the mid-ranges for non-GAAP gross profit margin and OPEX (62% and $43 million, respectively), I see an EBIT of $34.5 million. So, with a limited tax and interest impact, I see at least $0.19-0.20 per share in adjusted EPS. That would be a beat of ~8.3% if my math is correct. If so, the forward estimates will likely be further adjusted to the upside, leading to a lower P/E multiple for FY2026/2027, which is what growth investors want to see.

What I like in the current setup is CRDO's technical picture as well: the stock price fell off its ATH by ~35% and has touched its very important support zone of $46-50/share. While it might seem like an upward trend breakdown, I think it's not because a) the momentum in the business doesn't seem to be over shortly and b) we saw something similar in the past. After touching its previous support zone of $21-24/share and seemingly breaking down its uptrend, CRDO quickly recovered, driven by its impressive September 2024 report:

I hope that this time we'll see something similar to that September performance. The odds for another beating, as my calculations show, are relatively high today.

When it comes to CRDO's valuation, we might look at it from different angles, but in my opinion, for a growth stock like Credo, the most important metric remains PEG; to date, CRDO's non-GAAP forward PEG ratio is by 1/3 lower than the sector's norms, according to Seeking Alpha Premium data:

So based on my optimistic expectation regarding the upcoming earnings print, as well as the recent dip in the stock's price, I reiterate my "Buy" today.

Downside Risks To Consider

Last time I expected that Credo Technology has a massive margin expansion opportunity going forward, and my today's update indirectly leans on that take even though I took the management's mid-range guidance for fiscal Q3 2025 calculations. The likely margin expansion that I see would explain the valuation premium sustainability that I assume, and so here comes the risk: In case Credo can't expand its GP and EBITDA margins, my core assumption is broken in pieces.

Another risk comes with my Q3 EPS calculations. I assumed the higher end of the management's guidance for the quarter ($125 million), and so here I risk being too optimistic, as their past success isn't a guarantee of the future success. If the firm reports the mid-range figure or misses the current consensus, the current correction in the stock price will likely keep going. Even more pessimistic findings can be made if Credo misses on the non-GAAP consensus EPS.

Your Takeaway

Despite the risks, I still think that Credo Technology is a great AI pick in today's investment landscape. Yes, the stock's valuation may scare some skeptics off, but the ongoing expansion of AI and ML workloads in data centers is a major tailwind for the stock because the demand for the "high-bandwidth, low-latency interconnectivity solutions" - which Credo specializes in - is expected (by most third-party analytical agencies) to surge as AI infrastructure scales.

I expect another blowout quarter from Credo Technology. I'd be greedy and buy more stock if I had some additional capacity, so my "Buy" stays in place this time.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: March 2025 Monthly
data.description:
In recent weeks, while Russia's war on Ukraine continues and Beijing continues to harass its neighbors, the U.S. tariff threats and doubts that its defense commitments will be sustained, have emerged as the most significant challenge for businesses, investors, and policymakers. These tariffs are aimed at protecting domestic industries, forcing a re-shoring of production, and raising revenue. If, and when implemented, the tariffs can be expected to impact economic growth, inflation, and various industrial sectors. It signals a further break from the multilateralism of the past.

If the past few weeks are anything to go by, the transition away from the US traditional approach to trade and defense is unsettling as transitions often are. In the first instance, it has meant greater volatility, which in the capital markets, means risk. It also means uncertainty, and the lack of visibility is often associated with cautiousness on the part of business in making investment decisions.

Ironically, the changes that are being ushered in by the new US administration are most disruptive to US allies that have relied on the American market and military protection. So far, the Dollar Index, a basket of major currencies, peaked on January 13, a week before President Trump’s inauguration. It had rallied nearly 10% from the end of last September through mid-January. Profit-taking kicked, and even when the 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico looked imminent in early February, the Dollar Index was unable to set a new high.

Instead, “tariff fatigue" emerged as a dominant narrative as the Dollar Index fell 3.6% from the January peak. The tariffs were seen to be bluster and a negotiating tactic. However, as February drew to a close, Trump reiterated his intention to impose the 25% tariff on Mexico and Canada (except its energy exports), announced another 10% tariff on China (including Hong Kong), and indicated he was still moving toward tariffs on the EU, and industry-specific tariffs, on steel, aluminum, copper, autos, semiconductors, pharma, and lumber. The Dollar Index finished February on a firm note and reached a two-week high on the last day of the month.

We are still in the early days of Trump's second term. We suspect there is a profound shift taking place. We see the tariffs as part of a broader implementation of Trump's world view that is predicated on the US being a victim, which has been a common theme in both the mainstream and social media in the US. The US traditional allies are seen, in this framing, as having harmed it the most. In late February, for the first time since 1945, the US voted with Russia and against Europe at the United Nations on a European security issue. Some of Trump's advisors want to eject Canada from the Five-Eyes intelligence sharing group.

We argue that grand shift of the geopolitical and economic tectonic plates. The next 4-6 weeks risk even greater disruption. The 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico for border issues (undocumented migration and fentanyl) were postponed until March 4. On March 12, the U.S. has threatening 25% tariff on steel and aluminum imports. Reciprocal tariffs could come into effect in early April, but more likely will have a later start date. President Trump also has signaled that tariffs on auto imports may could be announced on April 2. As of now, the only tariff that has been imposed was an additional 10% levy on all imports from China and Hong Kong).

The economic consequences of tariffs are multifaceted and complex. Sometimes, as we have seen in the past, they do a poor job of boosting the competitiveness of US industries, while creating space for domestic companies to raise prices. Yet, rising prices to end-users is only one, albeit common, result. Profit-margins of either the domestic producer or the foreign producer can absorb some of the higher tariffs.

The exchange rate could also bear part of the burden, but there are various forces that impact the currency market. The Mexican peso, and Chinese yuan, for example finished February stronger than they were at the end of last year. The Canadian dollar is the only G10 currency lower on the year as of the end of February, and that is a minor 0.25% decline.

Economists often argue that tariffs have contradictory economic impulses. They can be both inflationary in the first instance, and the higher prices weaken aggregate demand in the next. There is also risk of two shocks from the labor market. The first are what appears to be 1000s of government workers let go. The impact may begin showing up in the data soon but may be more of a Q2-Q3 story. Government jobs traditionally stabilize the economy during recessions by providing steady employment and income. The loss of thousands of federal workers reduces disposable income, adversely affecting local economies and businesses that depend on consumer spending.

The second is the impact of the cooling of immigration that began in the middle of last year., while the policies of the new administration may extend and accelerate the trend. In the 12 months since January 2024, foreign born employment rose by 1.9 mln (to 31.8 mln), while domestic born employment rose by 766k (to 130.6 mln). The increase in the labor force has been an under-appreciated tailwind to the economy. Growth, after all, is a function of aggregate hours worked and productivity.

For its part, cognizant of the criticism of its post-Covid response that it was slow to recognize the persistence of supply shock price pressures, Federal Reserve officials have indicated that they will be particularly sensitive to it now. Nevertheless, many officials anticipate being able to resume easing policy later this year. As February wound down, investors appear to have grown more concerned about an economic downturn than inflation. The derivatives market has around an 88% chance of the next Fed cut in June and at least one in H2 25. As recently as February 12, the market had the effective Fed funds rate a little above 4%. It finished the month a little below 3.75%.

By the time the Fed cuts again, the swaps market is discounting at least two more quarter-point cuts by the European Central Bank, and at least one cut by the Bank of England, the Bank of Canada, the Swiss National Bank, and the Reserve Bank of Australia and the Reserve Bank of New Zealand. The market suspects Sweden's Riksbank may be done cutting until Q3, while Norway's Norges Bank is expected to begin its easing cycle in March, with another cut likely in Q3.

Bannockburn's World Currency Index, a GDP-weighted basket of the currencies of the 12 largest economies (with the eurozone counted as one economy), appreciated for the second consecutive month after falling in the last three months of 2024. The rise in the BWCI reflects the unwinding of the dollar's gains in the Q4 24. The US dollar fell against the G10 currencies in the BWCI except the Australian dollar, which was off less than 0.1%.

Emerging market constituents did not fare as well. Three of the six fell and they account for a little more than 27.5% of the BWCI. The onshore yuan eased by around 0.55%, though the offshore yuan appreciated by about 0.45%. The Indian rupee fell by around 1%, and the Korean won slipped approximately 0.5%. With Trump having the US seem to side with Russia (accusing Ukraine of starting the war and must accept more territorial loss given the lives Russia lost trying to take it), it is little wonder that the Russian ruble was the strongest currency in that Bannockburn World Currency Index, rising a little more than 11%. The Japanese yen's nearly 3% gain was the largest of the G10 currencies, helped by fall of US rates and the BOJ's commitment to continue to normalize monetary policy.

U.S. Dollar: After the dollar's Q4 24 rally carried into the first part of January, it peaked before the Trump's second inauguration and trended lower through most of February. The postponement of the tariffs on Canada and Mexico supported the priors of those who believe the tariffs were a negotiating tactic and arguably encouraged a complacency that concluded that until implemented the tariffs were not to be believed until actually seen. Nevertheless, in late February, as Trump renewed his tariff threat on Canada and Mexico for March 4, the Dollar Index rallied the most in three weeks and settled February at two-week highs. US interest rates trended lower from mid-January. The dollar's recovery in late February took place even as US rates continued to decline. The US two-year yield settled below 4% at the end of February for the first time in four months. The 10-year yield slipped below 4.20% and traded through its 200-day moving average for the first time this year. Growth concerns were highlighted by Atlanta Fed's GDP tracker warning of a 1.5% contraction. In the middle of February, it was still tracking around 2.3% annualized growth. And this is before tariffs (except on China) are implemented and before the large-scale federal government lay-offs expected. Another wild card is the US debt ceiling on March 15. This could lead to a government shutdown. The economic impact is a function of breadth and duration. As of the end of February, none of the 12 appropriation bills have been signed. Only four times has the federal government been shut for more than one business day. During Trump's first term, when the GOP had a majority in both houses, the federal government was shut for a record 35 days in late 2018 and early 2019.

Euro: The euro recovered from the spike low in early February to almost $1.0140 when US tariffs on Canada and Mexico looked imminent. When they were postponed, and disappointing January US retail sales and a contraction in manufacturing output saw the euro recover but was unable to take out the January high near $1.0535. The risk is that the broad sideways movement of the past couple of months has alleviated the overbought technical condition and euro is poised to weaken. The momentum indicators are set to turn lower, and the US tariff threats may re-emerge as a key issue in the coming weeks. Europe is at risk of becoming marginalized by the US-Russia accord and the broader reversal of US traditional policy thrust. Europe, it is often said, grows by crisis, and the crisis spurred by the change in the US political discourse is of historic proportions. It may be sufficient for Germany to eschew its debt brake, and for the EC to consider joint bonds for defense spending. The market is highly confident that the European Central Bank will cut rate key rates by 25 bp in early March and again before the end of Q2. The current deposit rate target is 2.75% and the swaps market has near 1.80% at the end of the year.

(As of February 28, indicative closing prices, previous in parentheses)

Spot: $1.0375 ($1.0360) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.0300 ($1.0335) One-month forward: $1.0395 ($1.0375) One-month implied vol: 7.6% (7.4%)

Japanese Yen: A combination of softer US 10-year interest rates and higher Japanese rates helped lift the yen by over 3% in February. The US dollar peaked on January 10 near JPY159. It trended lower in most of February and traded to almost JPY148.55 in the last week of February, its lowest level since last October. The (50%) retracement of the dollar's rally from the middle of last September (~JPY139.60) was near JPY149.20. The next retracement (61.8%) is around JPY147.00. Still, the dollar proved resilient in late February despite the slide in the US 10-year yield. Moreover, the 10-year JGB yield has fallen in only two weeks since mid-December. The yield reached almost 1.45% in the second half of February. It has not been higher since 2009. Japan's January CPI stood at 4% compared with 3% in the US. The last time Japan's CPI was above the US was in 1997-1998. The BOJ is committed to continue normalizing monetary policy. Although market surveys show a preference for a July hike, the swaps market does not have the next hike fully discounted until early Q4.

Spot: JPY150.65 (JPY155.20 Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: JPY152.00 (JPY154.15) One-month forward: JPY150.10 (JPY154.70). One-month implied vol: 10.8% (9.6%)

British Pound: Sterling extended its recovery from $1.21 seen in mid-January and reached almost $1.2715 on February 26, its best level in two months. It overshot the (38.2%) retracement of its decline from the $1.3454 area seen late last September, which was found a little above $1.2600. We suspect the upside correction is over, and that sterling can pull back toward $1.2300-$1.2400 in the coming weeks. The Bank of England meets on March 20, but it is too soon to expect another rate cut, especially given the acceleration of wage growth and firm core (3.7%) and services inflation (5.0%). The swaps market has about an 80% chance of a May cut and another cut in H2 discounted. The British economy eked out 0.1% growth (quarter-over-quarter) in Q4 24 after it stagnated in Q3 24. Growth in Q1 looks a bit better around 0.3%. The UK will pay for its increased defense spending by further cutting its foreign aid program. It has offered peace-keeping troops after a ceasefire in Ukraine. The UK may escape of harshest US tariffs. According to its figures, it records a small bilateral trade surplus with the US, while US data suggests it has a small bilateral trade surplus. Also, Trump does not like the European Union and claimed on February 26, that it was formed "in order to screw the United States,” which actually had been encouraged by the United States, under both Republican and Democratic presidents.

Spot: $1.2575 ($1.2395) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.2400 ($1.2400) One-month forward: $1.2580 ($1.2400) One-month implied vol: 7.5% (7.9%)

Canadian Dollar: The US dollar spiked to almost CAD1.48 in early February before the American 25% tariffs (10% on energy) were postponed for a month, its highest level in more than two decade. Subsequently, the greenback's gains were unwound, and by mid-February traded down to a two-month low near CAD1.4150. The downside momentum stalled, and the US dollar looks poised to trade higher in the coming weeks. Canada is vulnerable to the new US tariffs in the coming weeks. The 25% tariff for undocumented migration and fentanyl may still be implemented on March 4. A couple of weeks later, the US has threatened 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum. In early April, tariffs on auto and lumber are threatened. Tariffs on lumber and copper would also adversely impact Canada. The Bank of Canada estimates that 25% tariff on Canada's exports to the US (10% on energy) could cut Canada's GDP by 3% over the following two years. The Bank of Canada meets on March 12, and the swaps market is pricing about a 50% chance of a cut, which is fully discounted at the April 16 meeting. Between now and the end of the year, the swaps market has two cuts fully discounted and about 40% chance of a third cut. Meanwhile, the Liberal Party's leadership contest comes to a head on March 9. Former Bank of Canada and Bank of England Governor Mark Carney is well ahead. Potentially facing a confidence vote shortly after parliament reconvenes on March 24, there is speculation that Carney could call for a snap election (possibly April 21).

Spot: CAD1.4460 (CAD 1.4540) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CAD1.4400 (CAD1.4425) One-month forward: CAD1.4440 (CAD1.4525) One-month implied vol: 7.6% (8.5%)

Australian Dollar: The threat of US tariffs in early February drove the Australian dollar to a new five-year low slightly below $0.6100. It recovered to briefly poke above $0.6400 on February 21, which practically met a technical retracement target from the Australian dollar's slide from last September (~$0.6940). The rebound was extended even after the Reserve Bank of Australia began its easing cycle with a 25-bp cut on February 18. However, the correction looks to have run its course. As the market's worry turns to growth, the Australian dollar (and New Zealand dollar) under-performed. The central bank made it clear that it would remain cautious and the futures market does not have the next cut fully discounted until July. The market has about 60 bp of cuts discounted for the remainder of the year. Australia must have a national election by May 17. Incumbent parties in most high-income countries faltered last year, and the fate of the Labor government may be the same. It is trailing behind the Liberal-National coalition.

Spot: $0.6210 ($0.6220) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $0.6300 ($0.6255) One-month forward: $0.6215 ($0.6215) One-month implied vol: 9.9% (9.6%)

Mexican Peso: When 25% tariffs on exports to the US looked inevitable on February 3, the dollar spiked to almost MXN21.30, a three-year-high. However, the one-month postponement saw the greenback's gains unwind and the month's low was set near MXN20.20 on February 19. The tariffs for undocumented migration and fentanyl may still come into effect on March 4. Also, Mexico is vulnerable to sectoral tariffs, especially on steel and autos, which could be forthcoming in the coming weeks. Mexico's economy is weak. It contracted by 0.6% in Q4 24 and appears to have little momentum to start the new year. Yet, with inflation back within the 3% +/- 1% target range, the central bank has scope to continue to cut its overnight rate. After five quarter-point cuts in 2024, Banxico delivered a 50 bp in February. It kept door open to another 50 bp cut at the March 27 meeting. By then the US tariff picture would be clearer. As the peso recovered in recent weeks, the benchmark three-month implied volatility has fallen from almost 15% to around 12.5%, the lower end of where it has traded over the last eight months. For the right type of exposure, options may offer an attractive alternative to the forward.

Spot: MXN20.55 (MXN20.68) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: MXN20.75 (MXN20.71) One-month forward: MXN20.75 (MXN20.76) One-month implied vol: 13.3% (14.7%)

Chinese Yuan: Chinese officials do not appear to have tried offsetting the 10% US tariff imposed in early February by depreciating the yuan. The onshore yuan fell less than 0.2% in February, while the offshore yuan appreciated by about 0.75%. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate in a narrow range in recent weeks, though it has crept up in late February. Beijing's efforts to support the equity market is not generating strong results, as the CSI 300 is off 1.1% year-to-date, while the MSCI Asia Pacific Index's is up about 3%. However, the index of mainland shares that trade in Hong Kong is up almost 15.5%. China's 10-year yield discount to the US has fallen from a record near 320 bp in mid-January to less than 250 bp at the end of February. While the fall in house prices is slowing and disinflation forces among consumer prices are lessening, the Chinese economy appears to require more stimulus if the economy is to grow around 5% this year. The National People's Congress runs from March 5 through March 11 and may lay the groundwork.

Spot: CNY7.2785 (CNY7.2445) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CNY7.3500 (CNY7.5025) One-month forward: CNY7.1855 (CNY7.2425) One-month implied vol: 4.8% (4.8%)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Schrödinger: Still Waiting On A Catalyst
data.description:
Schrödinger's (NASDAQ:SDGR) Q4 2024 results and 2025 guidance were solid. The recent Novartis collaboration is also supportive of Schrödinger's approach to drug discovery and will help to minimize cash burn in coming years. Despite these positives, Schrödinger's share price remains relatively low, which is likely a result of the company's modest growth and large losses.

I previously suggested that Schrödinger was likely to return to YoY growth and its operating expenses were likely to stabilize. This proved to be the case in Q4, and guidance suggests this will remain the case going forward. While this is positive, I continue to believe that positive clinical data will be needed for the stock to rise significantly from current levels.

Market Conditions

Biopharma R&D spend remains under pressure for a variety of reasons, including:

  • The Inflation Reduction Act (particularly large impact on small molecules)
  • Elevated interest rates
  • Economic uncertainty

Trump's election has also created uncertainty in regard to government healthcare policy and funding. Elevated interest rates and access to funding is perhaps the most important determinant of R&D spend at this point, meaning companies exposed to this will likely need to see significantly lower rates before their businesses fully recover. In addition to pulling back on overall R&D spend, some companies have also shifted focus toward late-stage products, further harming companies like Schrödinger that are focused on drug discovery.

While the growth of Schrödinger's business has decelerated sharply over the past few years, metrics suggest this is only due to the macro environment. Schrödinger is seeing churn amongst smaller customers, although churn amongst larger customers remains low.

China and government spending are likely to be areas of weakness in 2025, but Schrödinger does not have material exposure to either of these. The company's China exposure is a low single digit percentage of revenue, and its NIH exposure is less than 1% of revenue.

Schrödinger Business Updates

Schrödinger believes that the combination of physics and ML has created a powerful solution for drug discovery and materials design. While adoption is likely to continue organically growing over time, Schrödinger continues to invest in the platform's capabilities. Planned releases for 2025 include predictive toxicology, enhancements to the platform's biologic discovery capabilities and additional integration of machine learning.

Schrödinger recently entered into a new multi-year, multi-target drug discovery collaboration with Novartis. Novartis also expanded its software agreement with Schrödinger. Under the agreement Schrödinger will receive 150 million USD upfront and will be eligible for up to 2.3 billion USD in milestone payments and royalties. The company has also expanded its collaborations with Otsuka and Lilly by adding another program to each agreement.

Schrödinger also continues to advance its proprietary pipeline. While these efforts are still nascent, Schrödinger has a number of clinical stage programs and has partnered some of its early-stage programs. Schrödinger expects initial Phase I data from all three of its clinical stage programs in 2025. Safety and preliminary efficacy data for SGR-1505 is expected in Q2. Enrollment is ongoing in Schrödinger's Phase I study of SGR-2921. The study is progressing well, and Schrödinger expects to report initial data in the second half of the year. Dose escalation for SGR-3515 is also ongoing, with initial data expected in the second half of the year. Schrödinger also has discovery programs in inflammation and neurodegenerative diseases. The company recently selected its EGFR program as a development candidate for osimertinib resistant non-small cell lung cancer.

Financial Analysis

Schrödinger generated 88.3 million USD revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 19% YoY. Software revenue was up 16% to 79.7 million USD, while drug discovery revenue totaled 8.7 million USD, an increase of 58% YoY. Large customers are driving software growth, as is hosted software. Maintenance revenue increased modestly, while services revenue declined 23% as existing services projects were completed. Drug discovery revenue was driven by milestones and the amortization of upfront payments from existing collaborations. The new Novartis collaboration wasn't a meaningful contributor in the quarter.

Schrödinger expects 10-15% software revenue growth in 2025 and 45-50 million USD drug discovery revenue. Software revenue is expected to be between 44 and 48 million USD in the first quarter, implying roughly 21% YoY growth at the midpoint. The increase in hosted revenue should reduce seasonality over time but is currently a drag on growth. Drug discovery revenue is expected to be weighted towards the back half of the year. While the Novartis collaboration will be an important contributor to growth, the increase is expected to be fairly broad-based.

Schrödinger's software metrics are indicative of a healthy business, particularly given the tough macro environment. ACV continues to increase at a healthy pace, driven by strong adoption and low churn amongst larger customers.

Software gross margins were slightly lower in Q4, which was attributed to costs associated with the predicted tox project, along with higher royalties. Drug discovery gross margins have begun to rebound though, despite an increase in labor costs as Schrödinger begins to move expenses from R&D to COGS, a trend that is expected to persist in 2025.

Schrödinger's operating expenses have been fairly stable in recent quarters, leading to modest operating leverage. While most of this is due to Schrödinger's pace of investment levelling off, the company has also begun to move R&D expenses back into COGS. Net cash used in operating activities was 31 million USD in Q4, down from 37 million USD in the prior year comparable quarter.

Schrödinger expects operating expenses to grow less than 5% in 2025, which should lead to a sizeable improvement in operating margins. Cash burn is also expected to moderate in 2025.

Cash burn is likely a point of concern for investors, but this should be viewed in light of the company's business model and strategy. Schrödinger has made heavy investments in its proprietary pipeline in recent years, which won't pay off for a number of years. Despite this, Schrödinger has been able to maintain a healthy cash balance and avoid excessively diluting existing shareholders.

Schrödinger ended 2024 with 367 million USD cash and marketable securities. This didn't include the 150 million USD cash received from Novartis in January. Including the Novartis payment, Schrödinger’s cash balance has only declined by around 75 million USD over the past 3 years. Dilution has also been fairly limited.

While cash burn is likely to remain elevated for the foreseeable future, Schrödinger should be able to continue offsetting this through things like collaborations and licensing.

Conclusion

It would be fairly easy to dismiss Schrödinger as an unprofitable software company that has resorted to drug discovery in an attempt to create shareholder value. There is a growing body of evidence that Schrödinger's platform can create significant value, though, and this is being amplified by advances in AI.

Schrödinger has a high margin software business that is growing at a solid pace against a weak demand environment. This may not be obvious from the company's financials, but it is evident from metrics like gross revenue retention and sales and marketing spend as a percentage of revenue. Looking at other simulation software companies, the software part of Schrödinger's business probably should be worth upwards of 2 billion USD.

The fact that Schrödinger's market capitalization is only sitting at around 1.5 billion USD suggests that investors are attributing little value to the rest of the business. Schrödinger has 3 clinical stage programs and at least 9 total proprietary programs, though. It also has 8 clinical stage and 2 FDA-approved partner programs. While Schrödinger's proprietary programs are still fairly immature, the success of companies associated with Schrödinger provides some confidence in the company's pipeline. Schrödinger's partner programs also mean that it is potentially eligible for milestones totaling over 5 billion USD, along with royalties on upwards of 15 programs.

Schrödinger's low valuation most likely stems from the fact that cash burn is currently elevated. Concerns over Schrödinger's cash burn are likely misplaced, though, due to the company's demonstrated ability to realize value through its partnerships and internal development programs.

While clinical success will likely be needed before Schrödinger's share price moves significantly higher, the company's ability to grow its software business, expand its pipeline and partnerships and manage its liquidity position without excessively diluting shareholders, demonstrate that it is creating value.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Back Below 6000
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 finished below 6000 this week for the first time in over six weeks. The index is now 3.09% below its record close from February 19th, 2025 and is up 1.46% year-to-date.

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

As of February 28, the U.S. Treasury put the closing yield on the 10-year note at 4.24% and the 2-year note at 3.99%. See our latest Treasury Snapshot here. ETFs associated with Treasuries include: iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF (SHY), iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF), and iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT).

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above, where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here're a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

Here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above its 200-day moving average since November 1, 2023, and below the 50-day moving average since February 24th.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. I've also included a 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. On December 18th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (3.45%) since November 30, 2022 (3.59%).

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks, spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 1.46% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 2.91% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/02/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.
